pathophysiology_summary: Erysipelas is a superficial dermal/lymphatic infection, usually Group A Streptococcus, producing an abruptly-onset fiery-red, sharply demarcated, raised, hot, tender advancing plaque with a peau d'orange surface, tender regional lymphadenopathy and prominent systemic toxicity (high fever, rigors, tachycardia). On the face this is high-acuity because of proximity to the orbit and cavernous sinus. The must-not-miss diagnoses are orbital cellulitis (eye pain, painful/limited eye movements, proptosis, visual change — currently absent), necrotising fasciitis (pain out of proportion, dusky bullae, crepitus, anaesthesia — absent), cavernous sinus thrombosis and sepsis. Discriminators from the butterfly-distribution mimics: a lupus malar rash is flat, non-tender and afebrile, and contact dermatitis is itchy without a febrile prodrome — the raised sharply demarcated tender plaque with rigors is characteristic of erysipelas. Because a telehealth clinician cannot examine eye movements/proptosis or give IV antibiotics, the correct action is urgent same-day in-person/ED assessment with parenteral antibiotics as needed.

differential.evidence_basis: Abrupt fever/rigors then a fiery raised sharply demarcated tender facial plaque.
Facial infection near the eye — screen for eye pain, ophthalmoplegia, proptosis, visual change.
Rapidly spreading painful facial infection with toxicity — exclude necrotising features.
Butterfly distribution, but lupus malar rash is flat, non-tender and afebrile.
Itchy not severely painful, no febrile prodrome.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Recognise high-acuity facial erysipelas, screen for orbital/necrotising involvement and sepsis, and escalate to urgent in-person/ED — do not manage this febrile facial infection purely remotely.

differential.evidence_basis: Classic erysipelas with SIRS; orbital and necrotising features currently negative but require in-person confirmation.
Cannot be excluded remotely; remains the key safety-net if eye features evolve.
High fever, rigors, extreme pain and raised tender borders exclude a lupus malar rash.
Working diagnosis: urgent same-day in-person/ED assessment for antibiotics (likely IV), sepsis screening, and orbital/necrotising exclusion.

red_flags.description: Systemic toxicity (high fever, rigors, tachycardia) with a rapidly advancing facial infection
red_flags.clinical_significance: Indicates significant streptococcal infection with sepsis risk on the face.

red_flags.description: Orbital involvement: eye pain, painful/limited eye movements, proptosis, or visual change
red_flags.clinical_significance: Orbital cellulitis/cavernous sinus thrombosis are sight- and life-threatening and cannot be examined remotely.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Vision loss or cavernous sinus thrombosis.

red_flags.description: Necrotising features (pain out of proportion, dusky bullae, crepitus, anaesthesia)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Necrotising fasciitis is a surgical emergency.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Anchoring on the butterfly distribution and missing an acute febrile bacterial infection.
Managing a toxic facial infection with home oral antibiotics and routine follow-up rather than urgent in-person assessment.
Not screening for eye or necrotising features that mandate emergency care.

cannot_determine_via_chat: IV antibiotic administration and sepsis monitoring
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Urgent same-day in-person/ED assessment for antibiotics (likely IV), sepsis screening and orbital/necrotising exclusion; ambulance if orbital, necrotising or sepsis features develop.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Managing a toxic facial erysipelas remotely with oral antibiotics, or missing orbital/necrotising involvement.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Erysipelas/facial cellulitis with systemic toxicity or near the orbit warrants urgent assessment and often IV antibiotics; exclude orbital cellulitis and necrotising infection.
